speaker: interviewer
text: 1996. The survivor is Benjamin Bender, and this is tape three. You were telling us about a German officer who took a liking to your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. The German former [INAUDIBLE] officer. because he was in civilian clothing. He was half-- his face half paralyzed. I think he fought at the Eastern front against Russian. And then he came to this department in HASAG. For some reason he liked my brother very much. He liked him. He was talking to him almost every day. And he was leaving his sandwich-- well, ham, I remember-- ham, butter, and two thin slices of bread-- in a box of ammunition. And then he was winking with his eyes that you pick it up and didn't want-- nobody should see it. Because, you know, this was a very offensive thing, to help a Jew. But he-- he did it. He did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And an act, so and so, you saw this act of kindness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was something which was almost nonexistent, that they were what I called in my book, points of light. There were some people who were willing even to put their life in danger, and to help some. But there were only few.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like at this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory, it was so hard work. Physical-- physical work. We have to move the ammunition to the machinery back and forth, loads. And we are working like one week a day, and one week a night. And there was-- the parking was very huge, hundreds of working people. We have German supervisors. And we have also Polish supervisors there. I think the Polish supervisors were worse than the German. I have to mention the fact that one of them was jealous of the fact that my father-- my father, I'm sorry, my brother-- spoke to the-- the German. And he couldn't speak German. And one day he-- he pick up my brother, and send him to get to 25 lashes-- which was done for no real reason. And he came back, his body was green and black and yellow and then-- then bleeding. And this was what happened. But [INAUDIBLE] it was bearable. I mean the life in HASAG, there was selection at night. At night when everything was lit up. And then the supervisor, this German, used to check, you know, the faces if somebody wasn't in good condition. Some Germans did not like certain faces. Just by face. If he-- maybe he didn't like his eyes. Maybe he didn't like his nose. Maybe he didn't like his ears. This was the judgment of the master race.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but we survived all these-- all these selections. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was already, we are talking about the end of '44. There were already rumors and good news coming from the Eastern front that the German army is in full retreat. The Russian are moving very quickly, especially the Eastern Front collapsed. And maybe a matter of weeks, or maybe a matter of months, we will be liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the moment came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But unfortunate there was also a moment when the freedom was close, but it was always far away. We were working at the night shift. In other words, we are in-- in the back-- concentrated-- all the maybe 2,000 people. Inmates. Jews. Those Jews working the daytime were dispersed all over the-- the factory, which was a huge place, a small town. So the German had a control, totally, about the Jews being concentrated, working at the-- at the nighttime, being in the barracks. On April 15-- on January 15, January 15, 1945, the German took, evacuated, over 2,000 Jews. We didn't know where we were going. One of the German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] SS people, official or SS, he said to us, you are-- we will save your life. The Russian are coming, and they will kill you, because you are working for us, and you are collaborating [? for. ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were herded [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to a freight cars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got a piece of bread. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I recall walking, walking to the waiting train, the locomotive puffing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hearing the shouts, of liberation, and the freedom being so close, and so far away. And we were walking to a field covered with snow-- young people, strong-- escorted by two old Germans, maybe in their 60s. At the end of the war, when the war was coming to an end, there was a shortage of manpower. So the German were [? not ?] taking old people. 60, 65. 55.

speaker: interviewer
text: I recall crying, looking at my brother, who was pale. His face, stone. And I say why, not one person, from all these strong guys, is trying to escape? One leader, and we will follow him. We will run, they could kill 5, 10, 20, but 2,000 they couldn't. They couldn't even have enough manpower. No movement.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we reached the train. And this was the last moment of the freedom was so close, but so far away. The train-- the freight car was packed like sardines. There was a pail in the center for human waste. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The people were hostile to each other. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everybody fighting for a space. Some wanted a corner, which was the best. But how many corners are in a freight car? So it was in the center who were in the-- in the worst way, because they are swaying back and forth. If some-- someone wanted to use the facilities, the pail, it was always impossible. It was always impossible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One of this-- our friend wanted to see what's going on around him. And he asks me, ask my brother, to use our hands as a ladder, and to lift him up. I think he had a screwdriver. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was trying to-- to get some view around, what's going on. Then people in the-- in the car began to scream, you are endangering your life. If you try to escape, you cannot do it, because we are counted. Well, I don't know what he had in mind. Maybe he had in mind to escape, because he escaped from a train from Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, this was end of the story. We arrived in-- I recall looking through the, it was a little view. I could see like a little opening. So I saw Chemnitz was the town. German town. We saw Breslau; Breslau, Chemnitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we arrived to Weimar. Weimar, it's five miles from Buchenwald. a historical town. A birthday place of Wolfgang Goethe-- a writer, humanitarian, philosopher. He wrote-- he wrote about the Weltschmerz-- about the pain of humanity. He wrote about people, about the indifference of people, and about the poverty. and about rejections. He was a German. And I saw this place. I didn't know still about Buchenwald. And then we were transferred from this train. When they opened the door, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many people were frozen to-- to death. I didn't count, but there were maybe 10, 15-- maybe more. We jump out to another train which took us to Buchenwald. Buchenwald is five miles from Weimar, located in Thuringen state on Ettersberg heights-- hills-- surrounded by wooded area. A real concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I learned later the span of life [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was between 30 and 45 days. When we arrived to this place, and I saw Buchenwald [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we crossed the gate, I saw the inscription. Then I understood the substance of human nature. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That people-- not everybody's good. Not everybody's mean. But people to people, somehow-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there is a Latin saying, which says, homini-- homini homini lupus-- homo homini lupus. It means, 'a human being to human being is a wolf.' This was said in ancient Rome, thousands of years ago. But probably it was true in modern times too. Me, my first question was-- there was a guy with a triangle-- a red triangle. He was a former Communist. He was one of the guys who was watching, you know, the people, you know, taking them to the bath house. I asked him the first question, if there is a gas chamber in this place. This was my first question. He said to me, there is no gas chamber in this place. There is a crematorium. And the dead bodies are burned after they die. People die, he said to me, mostly from starvation. This was my question. This was the end of the dialogue. I spoke to him in German. He was French, or Belgian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to this bath house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It looked to me very suspicious. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw water was coming from the ceiling, but there were no faucets. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, we were waiting. Probably it was freezing outside. It was January. It was 40 or 50 Celsius below zero. We are naked. Then we got a-- a burst, a splash, of ice cold water from the spray. And then after another couple of minutes, boiling water. And this was the end of the shower. There were no towels. Nothing else. Most of us were dry. We were given clothing-- not according to size. Some have shoes size two-- they got 12. Some have 12, got size seven, eight. The clothing was thrown to us, not given to us. Thrown-- boots in the air; clothing in the air. But by the inmates who are working in this store room. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have to explain to you now, because we are already in Buchenwald. And we went to a barrack number 62, which I remember the number. Buchenwald remains to this very day a big enigma. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The camp was divided in two. There was a big camp-- called the political camp for criminals. The political, I say, it was the ones who were Communist. There were people-- Jehovah's Witnesses. There were homosexuals, which have a-- wearing a pink triangle. The criminal have a purple triangle. The Communists have a red triangle. The camp at one time-- [CLEARING THROAT] the big camp-- had about between 50,000 and 60,000 [CLEARING THROAT] inmates-- mostly-- mostly non-Jews. Maybe a few hundred. The one who were Communist, who have friends maybe who tried to save their life. The small camp [CLEARING THROAT] was totally Jews and gypsies, about over 30,000. 70 barracks, 2,000 inmates in every barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [DRINKS] Capacity of 500. 2,000 instead of 500. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people from the shipment from before-- Hungarian Jews, Romanian, Czechoslovakian, but mostly Polish Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was a brewing hostility again, [CLEARING THROAT] between the Hungarian Jews and the Polish Jews. And you might ask why? [CLEARING THROAT] The Polish Jews have more discipline. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were forged much more by suffering than the Hungarian, who arrived in Buchenwald in 1944. We suffered from 1939. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But the people were dying. Every day [CLEARING THROAT] you have two roll calls [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 5 in the morning, when we came back from the work, the one who went to work. We stood for three hours in sub-- sub-freezing temperatures, our bodies in no underwear, no socks, pocketless slacks, no belts, our bodies blasted by arctic wind from the hard mountain. Some people, some inmates, were dropping dead. They couldn't stand these freezing temperatures. So I saw one by one, one next to me, one behind me. The snow was falling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The poles-- electrical poles-- were swaying in the wind. And the dead bodies of the people who dropped dead were being covered with snow. And they turned to little hills. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I stood with my brother back-to-back, and drumming our feet, rubbing our backs, trying to ignite our frozen bodies-- but in vain. I was dreaming about remote places-- about places having eternal summer. Because some places have eternal winter-- like Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: My brother asked me, what do you think? I say to him, I'm dreaming about beaches, drenched in the sun, about swaying palms, about blue sky. He said to me, you are a dream-- a dreamer. And you are a dreamer, I must just thinking about one thing, about the stinking bunk in our barrack. This was true. It was very pragmatic. Very down to earth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided to volunteer to work-- which was devastating. By taking in every day 200 calories, I was using up over 2,000. And i couldn't work in such a place, cleaning debris in Weimar for more than-- than one week. But I wanted to leave this place. This was a morgue. I wanted to see Buchenwald from outside. I wanted to see the faces of the evil. Wanted to see the German people-- their reaction to seeing skeletons walking to work. And I was hoping to find the scraps of food-- potato, or a piece of bread. But this was a total illusion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went. We were a group of 10 or 12. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We took the train from Buchenwald to Weimar. It was still dark. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were walking to a marketplace. And we saw many trucks, many German, in their summer, wearing the short, leather-- you know, the German Tyrol style, with the hat with the feather. And they were picking up the inmates to different places to-- to work. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I was at this place in 1991. But this is the story which I will tell us later. I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was working in a Reichsbank-- national German bank. There was a walking distance to this place, maybe half a mile. We went. And nearby the public park in which there is a monument of Wolfgang Goethe, with a beautiful inscription, 'To be humane, and to see-- to see the difference between the mean and the bad.' There was a German old man pushing a little cart. And on this little cart was a bag of potatoes and onions. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Suddenly, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] three or four onions rolled down from this cart on the ground. And one of the inmates darted quickly to grab the onions-- which he did. The German SS guard gave an order to stop. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he hit this inmate with his rifle butt in his face. The guy-- guy collapsed, the onions around him-- one of his hand still grabbing one onion. The German pressed his nail boot over his hand, and with the other boot, was kicking his face. In a matter of minutes, there was red pulp. No human face. Just-- a convulsive human body. Still alive. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We-- I-- I remember two guys. I was one of them. We tried to revive him. I took snow. I put on his face. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was-- his body was trembling, shivering. So he was alive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We-- we grabbed his body. The SS guard didn't say nothing. And we carried him to the bank. And we put him on the side.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to take a tape break now.
